my_sentence: My volume is too slow, I need to make it louder.
correction: My volume is too soft (too low), I need to make it louder.
explanation: 'Slow' is the opposite of 'fast' (speed). For a quiet sound use 'soft' or 'low'; its opposite is 'loud'.
correct_examples: Your voice is too soft; please speak up.
Turn it up — the volume is too low.
context: The teacher asking Jack to speak louder

my_sentence: Mrs Mary is very honest and she was not greedy anymore.
correction: Mrs Mary is not greedy anymore; she is now an honest person.
explanation: Keep the tense consistent — don't mix present 'is' with past 'was' in the same description. 'Anymore' means 'no longer / not at present', so it goes with the present tense.
correct_examples: He doesn't smoke anymore; he is much healthier now.
She is not shy anymore.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'greedy'

my_sentence: Mr White is a snob person.
correction: Mr White is a snob. / Mr White is a snobbish person.
explanation: 'Snob' is a noun, so it can't directly modify 'person'. Either use the noun alone ('is a snob') or use the adjective form ('a snobbish person').
correct_examples: He is a snob.
She has a snobbish attitude.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'snob'

my_sentence: No one become his real friend.
correction: No one becomes his real friend.
explanation: 'No one' is singular (like 'he/she'), so the present-tense verb takes -s: 'becomes'.
correct_examples: No one knows the answer.
Nobody wants to help him.

my_sentence: Last week I met a stranger who is a snob.
correction: Last week I met a stranger who was a snob, and I couldn't communicate with him, so I left him right away.
explanation: 'Last week' marks past time, so use the past tense 'was', not the present 'is', inside the relative clause.
correct_examples: Last week I met a man who was very rude.
Yesterday I saw a stranger who was lost.

my_sentence: He always looked down other people at work.
correction: He always looks down on other people at work.
explanation: The phrasal verb is 'look down on someone' (to think you are better than them); don't drop 'on'. For a general trait use the present 'looks'.
correct_examples: She looks down on people who are poor.
Don't look down on others.

my_sentence: Many professors and students will come to here to join.
correction: Many professors and students will come here to join.
explanation: 'Here' and 'there' are adverbs of place and don't take 'to'. Say 'come here', not 'come to here'.
correct_examples: Come here, please.
Many people came here for the event.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'discourse'

my_sentence: Many people who lived in the same neighbourhood with Mr John think he is arrogant and conceited.
correction: Many people who lived in the same neighbourhood as Mr John thought he was arrogant and conceited.
explanation: Match the tenses: since 'lived' is past, use 'thought' and 'was', not the present 'think/is'. (Also 'the same ... as', not 'with'.)
correct_examples: The people who worked with him thought he was kind.
Those who lived there said it was quiet.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'conceited'

my_sentence: We need to keep a bottom line so I did not hurt others.
correction: We need to keep a bottom line so as not to hurt others.
explanation: To express purpose ('in order not to'), use 'so as not to + base verb', not 'so I did not'.
correct_examples: He spoke softly so as not to wake the baby.
We left early so as not to be late.

my_sentence: I have to hard work to finish my dream.
correction: I have to work hard to finish my dream.
explanation: 'Work' is the verb and 'hard' is the adverb after it: 'work hard'. 'Hard work' is a noun phrase and can't follow 'have to'. (You made this same slip the next day — watch for it.)
correct_examples: I have to work hard to reach my goal.
They worked hard for years.
context: Talking about achieving his dreams with enough money

my_sentence: At least they need to graduate from famous college.
correction: At least they need to graduate from a famous college. / ...from famous colleges.
explanation: 'College' is countable. Use 'a famous college' for one, or the plural 'famous colleges' for several. Don't leave a singular countable noun bare.
correct_examples: She graduated from a famous college.
They attended famous colleges abroad.

my_sentence: When I came to Japan I can earn two salaries during that time.
correction: When I came to Japan I could earn two salaries during that time.
explanation: For a past ability use the past modal 'could', not the present 'can'. The time phrase 'during that time' is in the past.
correct_examples: Back then I could save a lot.
When I lived there, I could speak the language.
context: Earning two salaries while working in Japan

my_sentence: There are many people who like him come out nowadays.
correction: There are many people who are like him appearing nowadays.
explanation: To mean 'similar to him', use 'be like': 'who are like him' (don't drop 'are'). Also, for a person emerging/becoming known, use 'appear', not 'come out'.
correct_examples: There are many leaders who are like her today.
New talents appear every year.
context: Whether another businessman like Jack Ma could emerge

explanation: 'Taobao' is a singular subject, so the present-tense verb takes -s: 'belongs'.
correct_examples: This account belongs to me.
The brand belongs to a big company.

my_sentence: His net worth is about 28 billion dollar.
correction: His net worth is about 28 billion dollars.
explanation: With a plural amount, use 'dollars'. After a number greater than one the countable noun must be plural.
correct_examples: It costs 50 dollars.
He is worth billions of dollars.

correction: I didn't search for this information.
explanation: 'Information' is uncountable — it has no plural 'informations' and takes no 'a'. Also use 'search for' when you mean to look something up.
correct_examples: I searched for information online.
There isn't much information about it.
context: Jack saying he hadn't looked something up

my_sentence: Many products from the US and UK made in China.
correction: Many products from the US and UK were made in China.
explanation: This is passive voice and needs 'be' + past participle: 'were made'. Don't drop the auxiliary 'were'.
correct_examples: These goods were made in China.
The phone was designed in the US but made in China.
context: Talking about where products are manufactured

my_sentence: Maybe we can talk it later.
correction: Maybe we can talk about it later.
explanation: Use 'talk about something'. The verb 'talk' needs the preposition 'about' before the topic.
correct_examples: Let's talk about it tomorrow.
We talked about the project.
